A teammate insists `diffSnapshots` emits a `moveNode` op when a task node is reparented or reordered between two frames, and a separate remove+add when the root changes. Is that right? Explain what ops it actually produces for a node that moved, and what single op it uses when the root node's identity or shape changes.

verify.rubric: There is NO moveNode op. A node whose parentId or sibling index changed (a move) OR whose shape (type/name/depth) changed (a replace) is encoded as a removeNode of its old id PLUS an addNode at the new parent/index — the diff models move and replace identically as remove+add. When the ROOT's id changed, or the root's shape changed, the diff returns a single `replaceRoot` op (because the root node cannot be removed), not a remove+add pair. A correct answer must reject moveNode, describe remove+add for moved/replaced nodes, and name replaceRoot for the root case.

In the DevTools run store, a run flipped to `waiting-approval` when one task raised NodeWaitingApproval; a second task is parked on `waiting-timer`. The timer task then resumes (a NodeStarted event arrives for it). What does the store set the RUN-level status to, and what rule decides which waiting status the run shows while multiple tasks are blocked? Also: can this logic ever overwrite a `finished` run?

verify.rubric: When a task leaves a waiting state, the store recomputes the run's waiting status (refreshRunWaitingStatus), but ONLY when the run is currently parked in a run-level waiting status (waiting-approval / waiting-timer) and is NOT terminal. It scans all tasks: if any task is still `waiting-approval`, the run stays `waiting-approval` (approval is the strongest block and overrides timer); otherwise if any task is `waiting-timer`, the run is `waiting-timer`; if none are blocking, the run resumes to `running`. So here the run STAYS `waiting-approval` (the approval task is still waiting) — it does not flip to running just because the timer task resumed. Terminal statuses (finished/failed/cancelled) are never overwritten by this logic.

input.task: In the DevTools run store, a run flipped to `waiting-approval` when one task raised NodeWaitingApproval; a second task is parked on `waiting-timer`. The timer task then resumes (a NodeStarted event arrives for it). What does the store set the RUN-level status to, and what rule decides which waiting status the run shows while multiple tasks are blocked? Also: can this logic ever overwrite a `finished` run?

verify.rubric: When a task leaves a waiting state, the store recomputes the run's waiting status (refreshRunWaitingStatus), but ONLY when the run is currently parked in a run-level waiting status (waiting-approval / waiting-timer) and is NOT terminal. It scans all tasks: if any task is still `waiting-approval`, the run stays `waiting-approval` (approval is the strongest block and overrides timer); otherwise if any task is `waiting-timer`, the run is `waiting-timer`; if none are blocking, the run resumes to `running`. So here the run STAYS `waiting-approval` (the approval task is still waiting) — it does not flip to running just because the timer task resumed. Terminal statuses (finished/failed/cancelled) are never overwritten by this logic.

So here the run STAYS `waiting-approval` (the approval task is still waiting) — it does not flip to running just because the timer task resumed. Terminal statuses (finished/failed/cancelled) are never overwritten by this logic.

My observability policies have been firing alerts during a long run. Which 'smithers alerts' action lists the current alert instances, and which statuses does it include by default? Also: 'smithers alerts' is action-based, so what is the general shape of the command?

verify.rubric: Correct: 'smithers alerts list'. The command shape is 'smithers alerts <action> [<id>]' where action is one of list | ack | resolve | silence. 'list' returns alert instances in statuses firing, acknowledged, and silenced (up to ~200 rows); --format json/jsonl gives machine output. Must not invent a singular 'smithers alert' command or a per-status flag.

input.task: 'smithers alerts list' shows a firing alert with id 'al-77c2'. I want to acknowledge it (mark it as seen, but not resolved). Give the exact command.
'smithers alerts list' shows a firing alert with id 'al-77c2'. I want to acknowledge it (mark it as seen, but not resolved). Give the exact command.
'smithers alerts list' shows a firing alert with id 'al-77c2'. I want to acknowledge it (mark it as seen, but not resolved). Give the exact command.
What is the difference between 'smithers alerts resolve <id>' and 'smithers alerts silence <id>', and when would I use each on a firing alert 'al-77c2'?

verify.rubric: Correct: 'resolve' marks the alert resolved (the condition is over / handled; it stamps resolvedAt) via 'smithers alerts resolve al-77c2'. 'silence' suppresses/mutes the alert instance WITHOUT marking it resolved (still tracked, just quieted) via 'smithers alerts silence al-77c2'. ack only acknowledges (seen). A correct answer maps resolve -> close-out and silence -> mute, and takes the alert id as a positional argument.

A provider integration emits raw CLI JSON stream rows for Claude Code and Codex. Explain the Smithers canonical agent trace event shape it should normalize into before event-bus emission, including required run/node/attempt fields, event kind/phase/sequence, source capture metadata, completeness, payload/raw, redaction, and annotations.

verify.rubric: A correct answer must describe `CanonicalAgentTraceEvent` with `traceVersion: "1"`, `runId`, optional workflow path/hash, `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `timestampMs`, `event: { sequence, kind, phase }`, `source: { agentFamily, captureMode, rawType/rawEventId, observed }`, `traceCompleteness`, `payload`, `raw`, `redaction`, and `annotations`, and say it is emitted in `SmithersEvent` variant `AgentTraceEvent`. It should mention real kinds such as `tool.execution.start`, `usage`, `capture.warning`, or `assistant.text.delta`.

input.task: At the end of an agent attempt, I need one durable summary saying whether trace capture was complete and what the provider could not expose. What fields belong in the Smithers `AgentTraceSummary`, and which event variant carries it?

verify.rubric: A correct answer must name `AgentTraceSummary` and `AgentTraceSummary` Smithers event variant. It should include `traceVersion`, `runId`, optional workflow path/hash, `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `traceStartedAtMs`, `traceFinishedAtMs`, `agentFamily`, optional `agentId`/`model`, `captureMode`, `traceCompleteness`, `unsupportedEventKinds`, `missingExpectedEventKinds`, and `rawArtifactRefs`. It should explain this is separate from individual `AgentTraceEvent` rows.

input.task: Agent traces are not spans; we want them exported as OTEL log records with severity based on capture errors/warnings and rich attributes. Which builders/export helper should be used for canonical trace events and raw session transcript events, and how is severity chosen?

verify.rubric: A correct answer must mention `canonicalTraceEventToOtelLogRecord`, `agentSessionEventToOtelLogRecord`, and `emitOtelLogRecord`. It should say attributes include Smithers run/workflow/node/agent/capture fields and custom annotations; `inferCanonicalSeverity` maps `capture.error` to ERROR except truncated JSON stream is WARN, `capture.warning` and `stderr` to WARN, otherwise INFO; session severity is inferred from error-ish raw rows. It should not describe agent traces as only OpenTelemetry spans.
